does: This paper experimentally measures personalization benefits in social highlighting by using a co-readership identity control—observing the same documents highlighted by multiple users while holding document and topic constant. The authors demonstrate that a reader's personal history can identify which documents they marked versus other readers' marks, with identity-based advantage gaps of +0.169 against community baselines and +0.119 against topic-matched hard negatives. They find this selection signal operates consistently across document and span-level altitudes (ranging from +0.12 to +0.17) and is largely driven by stable thematic preferences rather than title-driven signals, suggesting personalization effects are robust but topic-dependent.
connects: This work extends prior research on next-document prediction and personalization evaluation that could only upper-bound identity signals due to methodological leakage from document-level confounds. The paper builds on the authors' earlier span-level selection work and connects to broader research on collaborative filtering and content-based recommendation systems. It responds to fundamental questions about whether personalization based on user history provides meaningful signal beyond document characteristics and community patterns, engaging with literature on co-readership networks and the separability of user preference from content effects in recommendation and highlighting systems.
enables: This work enables more rigorous evaluation frameworks for personalization systems by demonstrating clean identity-controlled measurement techniques applicable beyond social highlighting. The finding that personalization signal plateaus around topic preference suggests future research into what signal components remain untapped and whether other user dimensions (expertise, reading goals, cognitive style) could supplement topic-based personalization. The comparable signal across altitudes implies scalable personalization approaches, while the limits identified point toward studying where personalization genuinely helps versus where it may create filter bubbles or misrepresent collaborative knowledge, advancing understanding of the practical boundaries of user-centric versus community-centric information systems.
